title: When the Lights Come On
subtitle: What life looks like when AI becomes electricity
description: What life looks like when AI becomes electricity
body_html:
We don’t remember learning to stop marveling at electric light. There was no ceremony when the wonder became wallpaper. At some point, somewhere between Edison’s demonstrations and your grandfather flipping on the kitchen light at 6am without a thought, electricity stopped being a technology and became a condition. It became the air inside which modern life breathed. You no longer thought about it. You only noticed it when it was gone.

That is where AI is going. The question is not whether. The question is when, and what we will have lost or gained by the time we arrive.

Maurice Conti, one of the people who has been thinking carefully about this longer than most, calls it the fourth wave. After the excitement of the novel output, after the grassroots productivity gains, after the age of autonomous agents acting on our behalf, comes something quieter and more total. AI as electricity. Ubiquitous. Invisible. The infrastructure inside which everything else runs.

Most projections place this threshold somewhere between 2035 and 2045. Given that AI reached a hundred million users in six weeks rather than eighteen years, the honest answer is that none of our prior models quite apply.

We are navigating without a map that fits the terrain. But we can say something about the shape of the territory ahead.

The End of Friction

By the time AI becomes electricity, the question of whether to “use AI” will have become as strange as the question of whether to use arithmetic. It will be baked into every layer of the built environment. The hospital you enter, the school your child attends, the infrastructure of the city you live in, the supply chains that stock your grocery store, the legal documents your lawyer prepares — all of it will run on a substrate of artificial intelligence the way every one of those things already runs on electricity. You will not see it. You will not think about it. You will only feel its absence when something fails.

The primary experience of AI for most people will not be conversational. It will be frictionlessness. Things will simply work in ways they do not work now.

The wait. That is what will be hardest to remember. The three weeks for a biopsy result, the forty-five minutes on hold with the insurance company, the months for a building permit, the years a legal dispute takes to resolve — all of that waiting, which we currently accept as simply the texture of life, will be recognized by people in 2045 the way we now look back at the time before antibiotics. Not with nostalgia. With something closer to disbelief that we bore it as long as we did.

Personalized medicine will not be a premium option for the wealthy. It will be the baseline. Diagnostic AI will have access to decades of your health data, cross-referenced against hundreds of millions of similar cases, and will flag what a human physician would miss — not because physicians are incompetent, but because no human mind can hold that much at once. The physician in 2045 will not be replaced. But their role will have shifted almost entirely toward what AI cannot do: the conversation in the difficult moment, the integration of values into treatment decisions, the presence of one embodied person with another in the room where frightening things are named.
